clinical_coherence.notes: 28M Hispanic with confirmed HSV-1 encephalitis 6 weeks ago, now presenting with recurrent confusion and NEW involuntary movements (chorea, orofacial dyskinesias) not present during original HSV illness. The wife explicitly confirms these are new movements. The treating team's initial assumption of HSV relapse creates a realistic cognitive trap. Bilateral Babinski, seizures from the contralateral (right) temporal lobe, and catatonic features all form a coherent post-HSV NMDAR encephalitis picture.

tool_output_consistency.notes: CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis (32 WBC) with NEGATIVE HSV PCR (the pivotal finding), new oligoclonal bands (6 bands, up from 0), elevated IgG index. EEG shows extreme delta brush (never seen in HSV). MRI shows NEW bilateral temporal changes extending beyond the old unilateral HSV scar, with no DWI restriction or hemorrhage. NMDAR antibodies positive at 1:256. All outputs are perfectly concordant and create a compelling case for post-HSV NMDAR encephalitis.

ground_truth_quality.notes: Primary diagnosis of post-HSV NMDAR encephalitis is correct (ICD G04.81). Critical actions correctly emphasize repeat LP with HSV PCR, recognizing that negative PCR redirects from HSV relapse to autoimmune, and initiating immunotherapy rather than antivirals. The distinction that no tumor is expected in post-infectious cases is clinically accurate.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Masterfully calibrated diagnostic puzzle. The recent HSV encephalitis history creates an overwhelmingly strong cognitive anchor toward HSV relapse. The clinician must recognize that negative HSV PCR, new bilateral changes beyond the old unilateral territory, extreme delta brush (never seen in HSV), and new movement disorder all point to a fundamentally different process requiring immunotherapy, not antivirals.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 28M Hispanic recovering from HSV encephalitis is realistic. Post-HSV NMDAR encephalitis occurs in 20-30% of HSV encephalitis survivors, typically 4-8 weeks after initial infection. The 6-week interval is within the expected window.

red_herring_quality.notes: Three outstanding red herrings: recent HSV history (strongly biases toward relapse), left temporal encephalomalacia on MRI (prior damage may be blamed for all symptoms), and current antiviral medication (suggests patient is already being treated). Each is a powerful cognitive bias with clear correct interpretation.

educational_value.notes: Arguably the best teaching case in the entire set. Post-HSV NMDAR encephalitis is an important and under-recognized entity. The case teaches: (1) negative HSV PCR as the pivotal distinguishing test, (2) new bilateral changes beyond old unilateral HSV territory indicating a new process, (3) extreme delta brush is pathognomonic for NMDAR and never seen in HSV, (4) immunotherapy rather than antivirals is the correct treatment, (5) no tumor expected in post-infectious mechanism. The comparison MRI follow-up output is exceptional.

strengths: Post-HSV NMDAR encephalitis is an important and under-recognized diagnostic entity
The cognitive anchor of recent HSV history is a powerful and realistic bias
Negative HSV PCR as the pivotal distinguishing finding is a critical teaching point
Comparison MRI showing new bilateral changes beyond old unilateral territory is excellent
EEG extreme delta brush as never seen in HSV differentiates decisively
Contralateral temporal seizure origin proves a new pathologic process
The clinical pearl that no tumor is expected in post-infectious NMDAR is valuable
24-hour continuous EEG follow-up with 8 seizures adds clinical depth
